subsystem: ImageMagick DPX (Digital Picture Exchange, SMPTE 268M) image encoder. The function WriteDPXImage serializes an in-memory Image into the fixed-layout DPX file/image/orientation/film/television/user header blocks followed by the raster payload. It is the write/output side of a MagickCore coder module (coders/dpx.c).
provenance: Identified by the MagickCore API surface: the signature MagickBooleanType WriteDPXImage(const ImageInfo *, Image *, ExceptionInfo *), the DPXInfo dpx local, WriteBlobLong/WriteBlobShort/WriteBlobByte/WriteBlobFloat/OpenBlob/CloseBlob blob helpers, ThrowWriterException/ThrowFileException macros, AcquireQuantumInfo/ExportQuantumPixels/GetVirtualPixels quantum pipeline, MagickCoreSignature asserts, and the 0x53445058U ('SDPX') DPX magic. The sample banner 'primevul_c_train_2361_fixed' and the file name suffix cwe401 indicate this is the post-fix revision drawn from the PrimeVul corpus.
trust_boundary: This is an encoder, so the untrusted inputs are the properties of the source Image being converted to DPX, not a network wire format. When a hostile image is transcoded to DPX, the attacker influences: image->columns/rows/depth/colorspace/alpha_trait; the dpx:* string properties returned by GetDPXProperty() (filename, creator, project, copyright, film.*, television.*, orientation.* etc.); the 'dpx:user-data' profile blob (arbitrary bytes and length); and, less directly, image_info->sampling_factor and image_info->type from coder options. The hostile surface is therefore: (a) attacker-chosen string lengths flowing into fixed-size struct char arrays via strncpy, (b) attacker-chosen dimensions/depth flowing into size arithmetic and the quantum pixel-export buffer sizing, and (c) attacker-supplied profile length flowing into the header offset math.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point of the DPX writer. image_info carries coder-level options (sampling_factor, type, adjoin), image is the picture to serialize (dimensions, depth, colorspace, alpha, properties, profiles), and exception is the error sink. It returns a MagickBooleanType status (MagickTrue on success). All three pointers are treated as caller-provided; the properties and profile they expose are the attacker-influenced data for this review.

Local declarations. dpx is the large stack-resident DPXInfo aggregate holding all the fixed header structs (file/image/orientation/film/television/user), each with fixed-size char[] fields and fixed-length element arrays. value holds each transient property string; profile holds the optional 'dpx:user-data' blob. offset is a running MagickOffsetType byte counter used to know how many bytes have been emitted (so the code can pad to the image data offset). quantum_info and pixels are the pixel-export machinery. extent is the computed bytes-per-row for the raster copy. horizontal_factor/vertical_factor come from the sampling-factor option. Note dpx is uninitialized here; it is zeroed later at line 89.

Preamble. The asserts are debug-only sanity checks (compiled out in release). If sampling_factor is supplied it is parsed with ParseGeometry into geometry_info.rho/sigma and truncated to ssize_t horizontal/vertical factors; when no sigma is present vertical mirrors horizontal. The subsequent guard is meant to reject factors outside {1,2,4}. For chroma-subsampled YCbCr (factor 2) an odd column count is bumped up by one so the width is even. OpenBlob opens the output file for binary writing; on failure the function returns immediately (no resources yet held).

The whole dpx aggregate is zeroed, which is the single most important safety act in the function: it guarantees every fixed char[] field is NUL-filled so that later strncpy(...,sizeof-1) copies always leave a terminating NUL and every unset numeric field defaults to 0. The DPX magic 'SDPX' is written first. image_offset (where raster data begins) starts at 0x2000. If a user-data profile exists, its length is capped at 1 MiB, then added to image_offset and rounded up to the next 0x2000 boundary. offset accumulates the written byte count as WriteBlobLong returns the bytes emitted.

The DPX version string 'V2.0' is copied into the version field (bounded to size-1, terminator preserved by the earlier memset) and 8 bytes are emitted. channels is derived from the colorspace (1 for gray, 3 for color) plus one for an alpha channel. file_size is then computed as channels*columns*rows + image_offset, cast to a 32-bit unsigned int, and written as a header field. generic_size is a fixed constant describing the size of the generic header section.

industry_size is a fixed section-size constant. user_size mirrors the image_offset math: the profile length is added and rounded to a 0x2000 boundary. Then the file-header string fields are filled from image properties: filename, creator, project, copyright. Each follows the same safe idiom — fetch the property, and only if non-NULL strncpy it into the fixed field bounded by sizeof(field)-1, then WriteBlob the entire fixed-size field. FormatMagickTime fills the timestamp field bounded by its size. Because dpx was fully zeroed, unset or truncated fields remain NUL-terminated.

Continuation of the file header: project and copyright are copied with the same bounded strncpy idiom and written. encrypt_key is set to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF, meaning 'unencrypted'). The reserve padding block is emitted verbatim from the zeroed struct. This closes the ~2 KiB file header.

Start of the image header. image->orientation is mapped to the DPX 0..7 code with a default fallback. number_elements is fixed at 1 (one image element populated). The width/height guard checks whether columns/rows survive a round-trip through 32-bit unsigned int; if either exceeds 2^32-1 the write is rejected, because the DPX header stores dimensions as 32-bit fields. columns and rows are then written as 32-bit longs (not shown here but immediately following).

The DPX image header contains a fixed array of 8 image_element descriptors. The loop index i is a constant-bounded 0..7, so every array access is in range. For each element the low/high data and quantity fields are zeroed and written. Only element 0 gets a meaningful component descriptor chosen from the colorspace (CbYCr444/CbYCrA4444 for YCbCr, RGB/RGBA otherwise, or Luma when the image is detected gray). Elements 1..7 stay zero. Fixed indices mean no bounds hazard in this block.

The rest of each image element: transfer characteristic (PrintingDensity for Log colorspace), colorimetric, bit_size taken from image->depth for element 0 (truncated to a byte), packing set to 1 for 10/12-bit depths (used later to size the raster), encoding, and the crucial data_offset for element 0 which equals the file image_offset — this is what the later padding loop and the raster reader use to locate pixel data. Each element's fixed description char[] is written from the zeroed struct. All array writes remain fixed-index safe.

Representative of the orientation header block (lines 265-344). Numeric orientation fields (x/y offset, x/y center, x/y size) are defaulted to 0 and, if the matching dpx:orientation.* property exists, parsed from the string via StringToUnsignedLong or StringToDouble and stored in fixed scalar fields. String fields (filename, device, serial) use the same bounded strncpy(...,sizeof-1) idiom. The border[4] and aspect_ratio[2] arrays are filled by fixed-index loops from ParseGeometry results. All of this is scalar-into-scalar or bounded-string-into-fixed-array; no length-driven copy into an undersized destination occurs.

Representative of the film header block (lines 348-420). Each string field (id, type, offset, prefix, count, format, frame_id, slate) is re-zeroed with memset and then filled with the bounded strncpy(...,sizeof-1) idiom before its whole fixed size is written. Numeric fields (frame_position, sequence_extent, held_count, frame_rate, shutter_angle) are parsed from properties via StringToUnsignedLong/StringToDouble into scalars. The redundant per-field memset makes termination independent of the earlier struct-wide memset. No unbounded copy exists in this block.

Representative of the television header block (lines 424-502). time_code and user_bits are parsed with StringToTimeCode; interlace/field_number/video_signal/padding are single bytes parsed with StringToLong and cast to unsigned char; the many float fields (sample rates, gamma, levels, gains, break/white/integration) default to 0.0f and are optionally overwritten from StringToDouble. Every destination is a fixed scalar, so these are value conversions with no memory-safety surface beyond the correctness of the string parsers.

The user header id is written (bounded), then the raw user-data profile bytes are appended (length already capped at 1 MiB and folded into image_offset). Finally the code pads with zero bytes until the running offset reaches element 0's data_offset, i.e. the declared start of raster data. If a byte write fails it raises a file exception and stops. This padding is what guarantees the pixel data begins exactly at the offset the header advertised.

The raster-conversion setup. AcquireQuantumInfo allocates the quantum pixel machinery, including the internal row buffer whose pointer is fetched later via GetQuantumPixels; on failure the writer throws (and at this point no other resource is held). The quantum sample size is set to 32 bits and packing is derived from element 0. quantum_type is selected from colorspace/alpha/subsampling. extent is computed independently by GetBytesPerRow from columns, channel count (3 or 4), depth and packing — this is the byte count written per row and is the value that must not exceed the quantum buffer's real capacity.

For a detected gray image the quantum_type and extent are recomputed for a single channel. pixels points at the quantum buffer. The main loop walks each row: GetVirtualPixels fetches one scanline, ExportQuantumPixels serializes those pixels into the pixels buffer according to quantum_type/packing/depth, and WriteBlob emits exactly extent bytes from pixels. Every failure path (null pixels, zero-length export, short write, cancelled progress) uses break — importantly not a throw — so control always reaches the cleanup below with quantum_info still valid.

Cleanup. quantum_info is destroyed BEFORE the error check — this ordering is the crux of the CWE-401 fix: if the loop terminated early (y < rows) the writer throws, but because DestroyQuantumInfo already ran, the throw does not leak the quantum buffer. On the normal path the blob is closed and status returned. This is the corrected teardown sequence for this function.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Potential out-of-bounds read (latent, in the quantum pipeline). extent is the number of bytes WriteBlob(image,extent,pixels) reads from the pixels buffer at line 568, but pixels = GetQuantumPixels(quantum_info) is sized by AcquireQuantumInfo, which allocates from image->columns and the 32-bit quantum set via SetQuantumQuantum. extent is computed separately by GetBytesPerRow using the channel count (3/4/1), image->depth and packing. If image->depth or the channel/packing combination makes GetBytesPerRow return more than the quantum buffer actually holds, WriteBlob over-reads past the end of the export buffer (CWE-125), leaking adjacent heap memory into the output file. The safety invariant is: extent must always be <= the allocated size of the GetQuantumPixels buffer for the same columns/depth/packing. This depends on AcquireQuantumInfo and GetBytesPerRow agreeing; it should be confirmed that both use the same depth/packing/channel inputs so extent can never exceed the allocation. Since depth is attacker-influenced when transcoding a hostile image, this contract is the highest-value thing to verify.

Unsigned integer overflow (CWE-190). channels, image->columns and image->rows are size_t; their product plus image_offset is computed in size_t then truncated to a 32-bit unsigned int. The width/height guard at lines 175-177 only rejects dimensions that do not fit in 32 bits individually, so columns*rows*channels can still exceed 2^32 (indeed exceed 2^64 for pathological dimensions, wrapping size_t itself before the cast). The file_size header field therefore records a wrong, truncated size. Impact is limited because file_size is only a descriptive header field here — it is not used to size any allocation or copy in this function — so this is a correctness/interoperability defect rather than a memory-safety hole. The invariant a DPX reader must not rely on: file_size accurately bounds the file. Confirm no downstream code sizes a buffer from this field.

Weak validation gate (CWE-20, logic error). The intent is to reject any sampling factor outside {1,2,4}. Because all six clauses are joined with && (logical AND), the exception fires only when horizontal_factor is invalid AND vertical_factor is invalid simultaneously. If the horizontal factor is valid (e.g. 4) but the vertical factor is garbage (e.g. 3 or a huge truncated double), the first sub-expression (horizontal_factor != 4) is false and the whole guard short-circuits to false, so the malformed vertical_factor is accepted. The correct predicate is to reject when EITHER factor is out of range (an OR of the two per-axis 'not in set' groups). Impact is low: the factors only steer chroma-subsampling quantum_type selection and the odd-column bump at line 79-80, not a memory operation. Still, it means image_info->sampling_factor is not actually validated as claimed. Confirm whether a downstream consumer trusts these factors for buffer math.

Memory-leak contract on quantum_info (CWE-401) — the class this sample is named for, verified upheld in the shown revision. quantum_info is a heap resource acquired at line 527 and freed at line 576. The invariant is that EVERY exit between acquisition and end-of-function must free it. In this fixed code the risky exits are handled: all in-loop failures use break (reaching DestroyQuantumInfo at 576), and the final ThrowWriterException at 578 is placed AFTER DestroyQuantumInfo, so the throw cannot leak. The latent danger the reviewer must guard against on any future edit: inserting a ThrowWriterException/return between line 530 and line 575 (e.g. after SetQuantumQuantum or inside the loop) would leak quantum_info because ThrowWriterException returns immediately without freeing it. Confirm the ThrowWriterException macro's expansion does not itself acquire/leak, and that no maintenance edit reintroduces an early return in the acquired region.

Bounded but sign/width-sensitive offset arithmetic (CWE-190, low). GetStringInfoLength(profile) is capped at 1048576 by the guard at lines 97-98 before it is added to the 32-bit image_offset (base 0x2000) and rounded up to a 0x2000 boundary, so this specific computation cannot overflow the 32-bit field (max ~0x102000). The residual concern is that image_offset is written into the header and then used as element 0 data_offset (line 238) and as the loop bound of the zero-padding while-loop at line 513: if image_offset were ever smaller than the number of header+profile bytes already emitted (offset), the padding loop simply does not run and raster data would start at the wrong place, producing a malformed but not unsafe file. Confirm the 1 MiB cap and the boundary rounding keep image_offset >= the true header size on every path, including the alpha/gray channel variants.

Bounded string copies whose safety hinges on the struct-wide memset (defensive note, not an active bug). Every property-to-header copy uses strncpy(dst,value,sizeof(dst)-1), which by itself does NOT NUL-terminate when value is longer than sizeof(dst)-1. Safety here relies entirely on the memset(&dpx,0,sizeof(dpx)) at line 89 (and the redundant per-field memsets in the film block) leaving the final byte zero, so the field stays terminated and WriteBlob emits exactly the fixed field size without reading past it. This is correct as written, but it is fragile: if a future refactor removes the struct-wide zeroing or a new fixed field is added without a memset, the same strncpy idiom would emit uninitialized stack bytes (info leak) or an unterminated string. Confirm the zeroing at line 89 covers the entire DPXInfo aggregate including any padding-sensitive fields.

summary: This is the DPX image ENCODER (WriteDPXImage) in the post-fix revision. It is a well-guarded writer: the DPXInfo struct is zeroed up front, all attacker-influenced property strings are copied with bounded strncpy(...,sizeof-1) into fixed fields, dimensions are range-checked, the profile length is capped at 1 MiB, and — the namesake fix — quantum_info is destroyed before the final error throw so no CWE-401 leak remains. The residual risks are latent and lower-severity: a possible out-of-bounds read if GetBytesPerRow's extent ever exceeds the quantum pixel buffer, a 32-bit truncation of the descriptive file_size field, and a logic flaw in the sampling-factor validation that fails to reject a bad vertical factor.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Encoder entry point; carries the trust boundary but the signature itself has no copy/length contract.
Parsed into ssize_t scalars; the validation of the result is the actual issue, tracked at the next anchor.
All-AND predicate rejects only when BOTH axes are invalid, so a bad vertical factor is accepted (CWE-20).
Single increment on a checked dimension; no overflow of concern before the 32-bit guard at 175.
Constant magic written out; no input-derived value.
Explicit 1 MiB upper bound on the profile before any use; the guard is present and correct.
32-bit offset arithmetic bounded by the 1 MiB cap; residual correctness risk if it undershoots the true header size (low CWE-190).
Zeroing is a safety act; termination of all strncpy copies depends on it (noted in a finding).
Constant source, bounded to field size-1; safe.
size_t product truncated to 32 bits; header field only, CWE-190, low impact.
Fixed constants plus the same capped/rounded profile math as image_offset; scalar header fields.
Representative bounded property copy; safe only because of the struct memset — flagged as a fragility/defensive finding.
Length-aware formatter bounded by the field size.
Same bounded sizeof-1 idiom into fixed fields; covered by the strncpy fragility note.
Constant and zeroed reserve block; no input.
Enum-to-code map with default; no memory operation.
Correctly rejects dimensions that overflow the 32-bit header fields; a present guard, not a bug.
Constant-bounded index over a fixed-size array; every access in range.
Scalar assignments from enums/depth; data_offset propagation is covered by the image_offset finding.
String-to-scalar conversions into fixed fields; no buffer target.
Bounded sizeof-1 copies; covered by the strncpy fragility note.
Fixed-count loops writing fixed-size arrays from ParseGeometry doubles; in range.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: film header strncpy fields with per-field memset (348-418)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Bounded copies preceded by field memset; safe.
StringToTimeCode/Long/Double into fixed scalar fields; no copy hazard.
Bounded sizeof-1 copy; covered by the strncpy note.
Writes the profile's own bytes; length already capped at 1 MiB, no destination overflow.
Bounded by data_offset; if offset already exceeds it the loop is skipped (malformed but not unsafe), tied to the image_offset finding.
Heap resource whose free-on-all-paths contract (CWE-401) is the sample's namesake; upheld here but latent for future edits.
extent is the WriteBlob read length from the quantum buffer; if it exceeds the allocation it over-reads (CWE-125).
Consumes extent against the pixels buffer; part of the out-of-bounds-read contract.
Correct free placed before the final throw; the fix itself, not a defect.

findings.title: Possible out-of-bounds read: row WriteBlob length (extent) not tied to quantum buffer size
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (buffer size mismatch between producer and consumer)
findings.invariant: extent must always be <= the byte capacity of the pixels buffer returned by GetQuantumPixels(quantum_info); AcquireQuantumInfo and GetBytesPerRow must agree on columns, depth, channel count and packing so the per-row write never exceeds the allocation.
findings.failure_mode: When transcoding a hostile image, image->depth (and the channel/packing combination) is attacker-influenced. If GetBytesPerRow returns a larger extent than AcquireQuantumInfo reserved for the quantum export buffer, WriteBlob(image,extent,pixels) reads extent bytes from pixels and copies adjacent heap memory into the output DPX file (information disclosure / over-read).
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify that AcquireQuantumInfo allocates the quantum pixel buffer using the same depth/packing/channel inputs that GetBytesPerRow uses for extent, so extent can never exceed GetQuantumPixels' capacity for any depth (notably 10/12-bit packed and the gray-recompute path).

findings.title: 32-bit truncation of file_size header field (unsigned integer overflow)
findings.invariant: The file_size header field should faithfully represent the total output size, or downstream consumers must not trust it for allocation/bounds.
findings.failure_mode: channels*columns*rows is computed in size_t and then cast to a 32-bit unsigned int; the guard at 175-177 only bounds each dimension to 32 bits individually, so the product can exceed 2^32 (or even wrap size_t for extreme dimensions), writing a wrong/truncated file_size. No copy or allocation in this function uses the field, so impact is malformed output rather than memory corruption here.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no DPX reader (or other MagickCore code) sizes a buffer or bounds a copy from the file_size header field; if it does, that reader inherits an exploitable under-count.

findings.title: Sampling-factor validation accepts a malformed vertical factor (all-AND guard)
findings.bug_class: Improper input validation (logic error in guard predicate)
findings.invariant: The factors must be rejected if EITHER the horizontal OR the vertical factor is outside {1,2,4}.
findings.failure_mode: Because the six conditions are joined by && , the throw fires only when both axes are invalid at once. Supplying image_info->sampling_factor='4x3' leaves horizontal_factor=4 (valid), which makes the whole predicate false, so vertical_factor=3 slips through unvalidated. The out-of-range factor then influences the odd-column bump and CbYCrYQuantum selection. Memory impact is low in this writer, but the claimed validation is defeated.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether any consumer of horizontal_factor/vertical_factor (quantum export sizing, subsample math) can misbehave with an out-of-set value; if so, change the guard to reject when either axis is invalid (OR of the two per-axis groups).

findings.title: quantum_info free-on-all-paths contract (CWE-401) — upheld here, latent for edits
findings.bug_class: Missing release of memory after effective lifetime (resource leak)
findings.invariant: Every path from AcquireQuantumInfo (line 527) to function return must call DestroyQuantumInfo exactly once; no early return/throw may occur while quantum_info is live and unfreed.
findings.failure_mode: This revision is safe: in-loop failures use break (reaching the free at 576) and the final ThrowWriterException at 578 runs AFTER DestroyQuantumInfo. The latent hazard is that ThrowWriterException expands to an immediate return that does NOT free quantum_info; inserting any throw/return between lines 530 and 575 (e.g. after SetQuantumQuantum or inside the loop) would leak the quantum buffer — the exact defect class this sample was hardened against.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the ThrowWriterException macro performs no quantum_info free/acquire and that no error path in the acquired region (530-575) returns without first destroying quantum_info.

findings.title: Bounded strncpy copies rely on struct-wide memset for NUL termination (fragility)
findings.bug_class: Reliance on prior zeroing for string termination (defensive)
findings.invariant: Every fixed char[] field written by WriteBlob(sizeof(field)) must be fully initialized (NUL-padded) before the copy so no uninitialized stack bytes are emitted and the string stays terminated.
findings.failure_mode: strncpy(dst,value,sizeof(dst)-1) does not NUL-terminate when value is longer than sizeof(dst)-1; termination and non-leakage depend entirely on memset(&dpx,0,sizeof(dpx)) at line 89 (and the film block's per-field memsets). As written this is safe, but removing the zeroing or adding a new field without a memset would emit uninitialized stack bytes (info leak) or an unterminated field. This is a maintenance-fragility note, not an active vulnerability.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the memset at line 89 covers the entire DPXInfo aggregate (all header sub-structs and any fields not re-memset locally) so every WriteBlob(sizeof(field)) emits fully initialized bytes.

audit_checklist: Verify GetBytesPerRow's extent can never exceed the GetQuantumPixels/AcquireQuantumInfo buffer capacity for any attacker-reachable depth/packing/channel combination (10/12-bit packed and the gray-recompute path).
Confirm no DPX reader or MagickCore consumer sizes an allocation or bounds a copy from the 32-bit file_size header field written at lines 110-112.
Check the sampling-factor guard (72-75): it currently rejects only when both axes are invalid; ensure an out-of-set vertical_factor cannot drive unsafe subsample/quantum math downstream.
Confirm the ThrowWriterException macro does not free/acquire quantum_info and that no error path between lines 530 and 575 returns without DestroyQuantumInfo (the CWE-401 regression surface).
Confirm memset(&dpx,0,sizeof(dpx)) at line 89 zeroes the entire DPXInfo so every strncpy(...,sizeof-1)+WriteBlob(sizeof) field is NUL-terminated and leaks no uninitialized stack bytes.
Verify image_offset (and user_size) stay >= the true emitted header+profile size so the zero-padding loop at 513-523 never leaves raster data mis-located.
Confirm the profile length cap (>1048576 rejected at 97-98) is enforced on every path before GetStringInfoLength(profile) feeds the offset arithmetic and the raw WriteBlob at 511-512.
